titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 7 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/209

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7 April My p r Things become very easy for a b w with a little p r
to help him. I am more than reconciled now for I think Place
is good, & with a good GSO1, & a "very fair GSO2" the Division
will I hope not be let down by the staff ! It has again distinguished itself. There was another
scoup attack last night, and again our men played up & the
enemy failed to get in tho' it was our weakest unit,— you
can guess which I mean — I believe they did well. For the 4th
(? 5th) time the huns have failed with us but succeeded in the
next Division. I am afraid our men will not be altogether
sorry as the next Division treated us with "marked discourtesy>".
They are known as the "incomparables". The GOC when they came
offered to send up some of our experienced officers to teach them
the line they were taking over & which we had held.
Their GOC gladly accepted so our officers went up. They
arrived at the front lines about M Night & were sent straight
back with the retort "I don't think we have anything
to learn from a 6 month's division". GOC is very seedy. He really ought to go home.
Perhaps Farnham will be able to explain things to him,
& get him to do so, if he returns but I fear that may
not be for some time yet. Major Long is here now from the base. He had a
most awful journey living on chocolate for 48 hours. So
good for him to learn the discomforts of our men ! He too is anxious about future drafts for the Division I have to go for evening runs as naturally Place has
to go out to learn the front but if I can sit by an open
window all day I do not mind so much. I think Rex's would really be best for us if I get
home before May when Mater will be there, but this is
doubtful. Whether to go there in May I leave to you but
should be quite content. I do not think either very
likely but you might make all arrangements in case. I enclose Blaker's letter which please file. It
is a strange request perhaps but I like its frankness &
these are strange times. He has behaved nobly for he
was ever warning us of the war to come, & when it did
come the land of his adoption treated him disgracefully.
This I hope is in the past, & may there be a good future
before him, on which neither you nor I will look
with jealous eyes. The b w would like a prance with a p r it may
come some day. Your b w Wilfrid B Spender
